name: Zoom API
what_it_does: Zoom's REST API enables programmatic management of meetings, webinars, participants, recordings, and team chat. Supports creating and scheduling meetings, retrieving recordings and transcripts, managing users and accounts, and posting messages to Zoom Team Chat channels.
use_cases: Automatically creating and scheduling Zoom meetings from agent workflows
Retrieving meeting recordings and transcripts for summarization pipelines
Managing webinar registrants and sending invitations programmatically
Posting messages to Zoom Team Chat channels as an agent
Monitoring meeting attendance and generating participation reports
not_for: Real-time in-meeting automation (joining and controlling meetings requires Zoom Apps or SDK, not REST API)
Large-scale event streaming where purpose-built streaming platforms are more appropriate
High-frequency polling workflows (rate limits make continuous status checks impractical)
best_when: An agent needs to schedule meetings, retrieve recordings or transcripts, or manage Zoom users and webinars as part of a workflow.
avoid_when: You need to control or participate in live meetings programmatically — the REST API does not support in-meeting actions at the level of a real-time SDK.

auth.notes: JWT authentication was deprecated in 2023; all new integrations must use Server-to-Server OAuth (for agent/backend use) or standard OAuth 2.0. Server-to-Server OAuth uses client credentials flow with no user interaction required — well-suited for agents. Scopes are granular (meeting:write, recording:read, etc.). Account-level apps require admin approval within the Zoom account.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free plan: 40-minute limit on group meetings; API access included but limited to Basic plan capabilities
pricing.notes: API access is free at the account level, but capabilities exposed by the API depend on the account's subscription plan. Recording APIs require cloud recording, which requires a paid plan. The free 40-minute meeting limit also applies to meetings created via API.

performance.rate_limits: 10 requests/second for most endpoints; daily limits by API category vary; account-level limits shared across all apps

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error responses include a numeric error code and message string. Codes are documented in a reference table but messages are sometimes generic. Rate limit responses include Retry-After headers. Webhook payloads are well-documented.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No idempotency key support. Meeting creation is not idempotent — retried requests create duplicate meetings. Agents must check for existing meetings before creating.
known_agent_gotchas: JWT auth was deprecated June 2023 — agents using legacy JWT must migrate to Server-to-Server OAuth
Daily API rate limits (not per-minute) can catch agents off guard; burst operations can exhaust daily quota
Meeting join URLs expire and cannot be reused — agents storing URLs must regenerate before use
Recording download requires a separate authenticated download step; direct URLs expire after a short window
Webhook events require URL verification handshake on registration — agent endpoint must respond correctly
Account-level OAuth apps require an admin within the Zoom account to approve the app before it can be used

security_score_components.security_notes: Server-to-Server OAuth is solid for agent use. Webhook payloads include HMAC-SHA256 signature verification. Scopes are reasonably granular. Historical security concerns (pre-2021) have largely been addressed by Zoom's security overhaul.

score_rationale: Zoom API is adequate for scheduling and management tasks but has notable agent friction: no idempotency support, daily rate limits that burst operations can exhaust, and the deprecated JWT auth path creates migration risk for existing integrations. Server-to-Server OAuth is well-suited for agents once provisioned. Best used as a scheduling and recording-retrieval API; real-time meeting control is not supported via REST.
